selection_reason: Surge pricing depends on NON-LINEAR interactions between time, weather, and events. A tree-based model captures that '7 PM + Heavy Rain + Diwali = 3x surge' in a way that linear regression structurally cannot. I selected the final model by 5-fold cross-validation RMSE — not test set RMSE — to avoid overfitting to a single split.

Ridge (baseline).why_included: Ridge is the mandatory baseline. It tells us how much lift tree-based models add. If Ridge performs similarly, trees are overkill.
Ridge (baseline).core_assumption: Surge is a LINEAR combination of features — doubling rain doubles surge. This is almost certainly wrong for real demand data.
Ridge (baseline).why_it_fails_here: Surge pricing is driven by NON-LINEAR interactions. Heavy Rain at 7 PM during Diwali is not simply Rain + Evening + Diwali. These compound multiplicatively. Ridge cannot model this.

Random Forest.why_included: Random Forest handles non-linear relationships and feature interactions out of the box, without any feature scaling. It is robust to outliers and gives reliable feature importances.
Random Forest.core_assumption: Each tree independently learns demand patterns. Averaging 200 trees reduces variance without increasing bias.
Random Forest.why_it_wins: Best CV-RMSE in this dataset. Robust because it averages predictions — one bad tree does not dominate. Suitable when training data is moderate (~1500 rows).

Gradient Boosting.why_included: Gradient Boosting iteratively corrects the residuals of previous trees. This makes it extremely powerful for tabular data with complex feature interactions like time × weather × festival — exactly our problem.
Gradient Boosting.core_assumption: Each new tree specifically targets cases where the previous model was wrong. With 300 iterations and a small learning rate (0.05), it converges carefully.
Gradient Boosting.why_close_to_rf: On ~1500 rows, both GB and RF are similarly powerful. GB would likely pull ahead on 10k+ rows where its iterative correction shines more. CV-RMSE is 0.2428 vs RF's 0.2407 — within noise range.

feature_importance.business_meaning: Direct proxy for demand-supply gap. Longer wait = fewer available drivers = higher surge.
Festivals (Diwali/Holi/Eid) spike demand 1.8x. Unique to Indian market.
10PM–2AM: driver supply drops sharply. Surge nearly guaranteed.
Raw hour captures intra-day demand curve (office peaks at 8AM, 7PM).
Cyclical: ensures model knows 11PM and 1AM are 2 hours apart, not 22.
Binary festival flag. Strong categorical demand signal.
Festival intensity (Diwali=3 > Eid=2 > Regular=0). Granular demand signal.
6PM–9PM office return + dinner = highest daily demand window.
1–5 severity scale. Heavy Rain=5 reduces supply while increasing demand.
Interaction feature: rain DURING peak hours compounds surge multiplicatively.
Encoded weather category — captures ordinal weather patterns.
City-level fare tier. Higher-fare cities have different driver density.
Longer rides: drivers more willing → slightly lower surge at high distance.
Cyclical hour encoding (sin component).
8AM–10AM office commute. Second busiest window of the day.
Mumbai and Delhi have structurally higher base demand than Ahmedabad.
People abandon autos/bikes → Uber demand spikes instantly.
Whether ride was accepted — strong proxy for price tolerance at current surge.
Cyclical day-of-week (sin): Monday and Sunday are 1 day apart.
Heavy rain specifically — strongest single weather signal.
Cyclical day-of-week (cos component).
Raw day — Monday commute differs from Friday evening patterns.
Encoded segment: affects cancellation probability and acceptance rate.
Budget customers cancel at 1.5x. Premium tolerate 3x. Changes cancellation rate.
Weekends shift demand from office to leisure — different surge profile.
Sat/Sun 7PM–10PM: combines leisure surge + late-night supply drop.
